coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of modified glassy carbon electrode using graphene quantum dot, gold nanoparticles and 4-(((4-mercaptophenyl)imino)methyl) benzene-1,2-diol by self-assembly method and investigation of their electrocatalytic activities
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Mazloum-Ardakani, Mohammad
Aghaei, Roghayyeh
Abdollahi-Alibeik, Mohammad
Moaddeli, Ali
coredata.dc:description: Results of this paper related to fabrication of a simple, reproducible, stable and sensitive glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified by assembly of graphene quantum dot (GQDs), gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and 4-(((4-mercaptophenyl)imino)methyl)benzene-1,2-diol (MIB). The GQDs were electrostatically assembled on GCE and electrochemically reduced in 0.1M phosphate buffer solution (pH=7.0) with the cyclic voltammetry method at the scan rate of 50mVs−1 for 15 cycles in order to reduce the oxygen functional groups. These results lead to the formation of electrochemically reduced graphene quantum dots film on GCE (ERGQD/GCE). In the next step, electrodeposition of gold nanoparticles was successfully performed at constant potential of −0.2V (vs. SCE) for 40s on ERGQDs/GCE. The constructed Au/ERGQDs/GCE was characterized with scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and electrochemical techniques. Furthermore, to improve electrocatalytic activity and charge injection, a self-assembled monolayer of functionalized molecules was grafted on the electrode surface by immersion of Au/ERGQD/GCE in aqueous solution (1.0mM) of MIB. Finally, the electrocatalytic activity of MIB/Au/ERGQD/GCE was investigated by the simultaneous determination of glutathione (GSH), uric acid (UA) and tryptophan (Trp) using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). This modified electrode enhanced the oxidation currents of GSH, UA, and Trp compared to bare GCE. Under the optimum conditions, the calibration curve of GSH was linear in the range of 0.03–40.0μM and 40.0–1300.0μM and the detection limit of 9nM was obtained. According to the results obtained in this study, the electrocatalytic activity of the present modified electrode was highly reproducible and stable.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Gold nanoparticles
Modified electrode
Glutathione
Uric acid
Tryptophan

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: General procedure for the synthesis of 4-(((4-mercaptophenyl)imino)methyl) benzene-1,2-diol (MIB)
Electrochemical behaviors of glutathione (GSH) on the MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE
Simultaneous determination of GSH, UA and Trp
Application of MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE for recovery and determination of GSH in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Electrochemical reduction of assembled GQDs on GC electrode (15 cycles) in 0.1M PBS (pH=7.0) at a scan rate of 50mVs−1.
(A) SEM image obtained for graphene oxide (GO), (B) TEM image of graphene quantum dots GQDs, (C) UV–vis spectra of graphite (G), GO and GQD and (D) FT-IR spectrum of G, GO and GQDs.
(A) Absorption spectrum of AuNPs and (B) shows SEM image of the particles.
SEM images of Bare GCE (A), GQDs/GCE (B) and Au/ERGQDs/GCE (C).

Nyquist plots for (a) Bare GC, (b) GQDs/GCE, (c) ERGQDs/GCE, (d) Au/ERGQDs/GCE and (e) MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE electrodes in PBS (100mM, pH 7.0, 0.9% NaCl) solution containing 5mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− at scanning frequencies from 0.01 to 100,000Hz.

Cyclic voltammograms of MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE in buffer solution (pH 7.0) for different scan rates; the letters 1–19 correspond to: 10, 20, 30, 50,100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700, 800, 900, 1000, 1200, 1400, 1700, 2000 mVs− 1. Insets (A): plot of peak potentials vs. the logarithm of the potential scan rate in the range of 10–6000 mVs− 1, (B) I p vs. scan rate and (C) E pa, E pc, E 1/2 vs. pH.

CVs obtained for the oxidation of 0.2mM of GSH at (b) bare GCE, (c) GQDs/GCE, (d) ERGQDs/GCE, (e) Au/ERGQDs/GCE and (g) MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE. Curves (a) and (f) show the CVs of blank solution at the bare GCE and MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE respectively. Electrolyte: 0.1MPB solution (pH 7.0), scan rate: 30 mVs− 1.

Cyclic voltammograms of MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE in 0.1M phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0) containing 0.20mM GSH at various scan rates; the letters a–h correspond to: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40 and 50 mVs− 1 respectively. Insets: variation of (A) anodic peak current vs. ν 1/2; (B) normalized current (I p/ν 1/2) vs. ν; and (C) Tafel plot derived from the rising part of voltammogram recorded at a scan rate 15 mVs− 1.

Chronoamperometric obtained at MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE in 0.1M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0), for different concentrations of GSH. The numbers of a–f correspond to 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6 and 0.8mM of GSH. Insets: (A) plot of I vs. t − 1/2 obtained from chronoamperograms and (B) plot of the slopes of the straight lines against the GSH concentrations.

DPVs of MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.0) containing different concentrations of GSH. The numbers of 1–18 correspond to: 0.03, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0, 20.0, 30.0, 40.0, 70.0, 100.0, 200.0, 300.0, 500.0, 700.0, 900.0, 1100.0 and 1300.0μM of GSH. Insets show the plot of the electrocatalytic peak current as a function of GSH concentration in the ranges of 0.03–40.0μM and 40.0–1300.0μM.

DPVs of MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE in phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) containing different concentrations of GSH, UA and Trp: from inner to outer corresponds to mixed solutions of 5.0+15.0+20.0, 10.0+50.0+50.0, 15.0+100.0+100.0, 25.0+200.0+200.0, 50.0+300.0+300.0, 100.0+400.0+400.0, 200.0+500.0+500.0, 300.0+600.0+600.0, 400.0+80.0+700.0, 500.0+900.0+800.0, 600.0+1000.0+900.0, 700.0+1100.0+1000.0 where the first value is the concentration of GSH, the second value is the concentration of UA and the last value is the concentration of Trp in μM. Insets: (A), (B) and (C) are calibration plots of GSH, UA and PA, respectively; scan rate: 20mVs−1, pulse amplitude: 0.05V and pulse width: 0.05s.
Steps involved in the fabrication of MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE electrode.
Comparison of dynamic ranges and detection limits for voltammetric determination of GSH at the various modified electrodes.

CPE mod. with TiO2-ferrocene carboxylic acid

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Cobalt phthalocyaninetetrasulfonate intercalated Zn_Al layered double hydroxide/chitosan.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The application of proposed novel method for simultaneous determination of the GSH, UA and Trp in synthetic solutions and diluted blood serum samples and the amounts of GSH, UA and Trp in blood serum samples determined by HPLC technique.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of modified glassy carbon electrode using graphene quantum dot, gold nanoparticles and 4-(((4-mercaptophenyl)imino)methyl) benzene-1,2-diol by self-assembly method and investigation of their electrocatalytic activities

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Yazd University, Yazd, Islamic Republic of Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Construction of modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) which assembled layer-by-layer with graphene quantum dot (GQDs), gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and 4-(((4-mercaptophenyl)imino)methyl)benzene-1,2-diol (MIB).

Results of this paper related to fabrication of a simple, reproducible, stable and sensitive glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified by assembly of graphene quantum dot (GQDs), gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and 4-(((4-mercaptophenyl)imino)methyl)benzene-1,2-diol (MIB). The GQDs were electrostatically assembled on GCE and electrochemically reduced in 0.1M phosphate buffer solution (pH=7.0) with the cyclic voltammetry method at the scan rate of 50mVs−1 for 15 cycles in order to reduce the oxygen functional groups. These results lead to the formation of electrochemically reduced graphene quantum dots film on GCE (ERGQD/GCE). In the next step, electrodeposition of gold nanoparticles was successfully performed at constant potential of −0.2V (vs. SCE) for 40s on ERGQDs/GCE. The constructed Au/ERGQDs/GCE was characterized with scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and electrochemical techniques. Furthermore, to improve electrocatalytic activity and charge injection, a self-assembled monolayer of functionalized molecules was grafted on the electrode surface by immersion of Au/ERGQD/GCE in aqueous solution (1.0mM) of MIB. Finally, the electrocatalytic activity of MIB/Au/ERGQD/GCE was investigated by the simultaneous determination of glutathione (GSH), uric acid (UA) and tryptophan (Trp) using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). This modified electrode enhanced the oxidation currents of GSH, UA, and Trp compared to bare GCE. Under the optimum conditions, the calibration curve of GSH was linear in the range of 0.03–40.0μM and 40.0–1300.0μM and the detection limit of 9nM was obtained. According to the results obtained in this study, the electrocatalytic activity of the present modified electrode was highly reproducible and stable.

ce:section.ce:para: The self-assembly is one of the most accepted methods for the fabrication of nanomaterials on conducting substrates. Molecular self-assembly on solid surfaces is provided by the precise interaction between molecule–molecule and molecule–substrate interplays that can be tuned by varying molecular building blocks, surface chemistry and structure as well as substrate temperature [1]. An example of self-assembly phenomena is formation of monolayers of organic molecules on surfaces. This monolayers, self-assembled on the surface of a metal or a semiconductor electrode can improve the electrochemical potential of the injected carriers [2,3].

Graphene, a novel class of carbon-based nanomaterial, consist of a single layer of carbon atoms attached together by sp2 covalent bonds [4,5]. It is demonstrated as an individual of graphite which is produced of many graphite layers held together by vander Waals force of attraction [4,6]. However, electronic and opto-electronic application of graphene is limited because the graphene is a zero-bandgap semiconductor. Graphene sheets, which are smaller than 100nm, are called graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The quantum confinement and edge effects of GQDs lead to various electronic, optoelectronic properties, large surface area and high conducting nature. According to these properties, GQDs selected as excellent candidate for manufacturing of electrochemical sensors [7–9]. It also can be applied as an appropriate material for the construction of electrode surface to improve the electrocatalysis. There are some reports on electrochemical sensors constructed with GQDs [10,11], nevertheless only few reports were available in the literature for the fabrication of graphene quantum dots on substrates using the self-assembly method [12].

Electrochemical reduction of graphene oxide (GO) has attracted great attention in recent years, since it is considered as a green approach. Furthermore, a thin film of reduced graphene can be formed easily on the surface of conducting substrates by this method [13–15].

Electroanalytical chemists attracted to the gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) because of their good biological compatibility, excellent conducting capability, high surface-to-volume ratio, unique structural, magnetic, optical and catalytic properties. Recently many research articles using AuNPs for various electrochemical applications such as bioassays, biosensor, chemical sensor and electrocatalysis have been published [16–18].

Glutathione (GSH) and glutathione disulfide has physiological importance due to their implications in a series of medical conditions including diabetes, Parkinson’s disease and cancer. It is known that glutathione plays antioxidant role in the human body [19]. Moreover its ability to scavenge physiological free radicals is particular importance considering the nitric oxide release associated with inflammatory disorders [20,21]. Consequently, tolerance of physiological glutathione concentrations have been correlated with patients suffering from Alzheimer’s disease [22], Parkinson’s disease [23,24], diabetes mellitus [25], atherosclerosis [26], arthritis [27], epilepsy [28], aging [29], as well as numerous types of cancer [30]. Therefore, it is necessary for simple, rapid and cheap assays to aid clinical diagnostics. Various techniques have been used for quantitative GSH determination including high-performance liquid chromatography [31], spectrofluorimetry [32], spectrophotometry [33], chemiluminescence [34] and capillary electrophoresis combined with electrochemical detection [34]. All of these techniques require time-consuming sample preparation, costly equipment and trained person to operate. But electrochemical techniques exhibit advantages such as simplicity, rapidity, high sensitivity and low cost for the analysis of biological compounds including GSH [35,36].

Uric acid (UA) is a product of the metabolic breakdown of purine nucleotides. Physiological concentration of UA for a normal healthy person is within a certain range, and abnormal changes in its concentration level are symptoms of some diseases like gout, hyperuricemia, Lesch–Nyhan syndrome, leukemia, and pneumonia [37]. Among different methods used for the determination of UA, electrochemical methods are more facile, less expensive and less time-consuming than the other methods [38].

Tryptophan (Trp) an essential amino acid in humans and herbivores is a precursor to the neurotransmitter serotonin. The availability of serotonin in the brain depends on blood Trp levels, which can regulate the psychoneural control of spontaneous changes through presynaptic inhibition of hippocampal cholinergic terminals. Thus, being simple, sensitive and inexpensive detection of Trp has great importance. Accordingly various methods have been devised and applied for the determination of Trp [39–41], which the electrochemical detection has been of considerable interest in the case of electroactive compounds [42–44].

To the best of our knowledge, there have been no report on the fabrication of self-assembled monolayer (SAMs) by GQDs, AuNPs and 4-(((4-mercaptophenyl)imino)methyl) benzene-1,2-diol (MIB) for simultaneous detection of GSH, UA and Trp. In the present study, we described a simple and facile method for the fabrication of the GCE modified with GQDs, AuNPs and MIB as a novel electrode. Initially, a simple method for the first time is presented for making of electrochemically reduced graphene quantum dot (ERGQDs) films by the reduction of the assembled GQDs on GCE. Then AuNPs was successfully electrodeposited and finally a monolayer of MIB self-assembled on the electrode. The GQDs/GCE, ERGQDs/GCE, Au/ERGQDs/GCE and MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE modified electrodes were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and electrochemical techniques. The electrocatalytic activity of the MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE electrode was examined by electrocatalysis and determination of GSH. Afterwards, we evaluated the analytical performance of the MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE electrode with simultaneous determination of GSH, UA and Trp in synthetic solutions and diluted blood serum samples.

The electrochemical measurements and AC electrical impedance spectra were carried out with potentiostat/galvanostat Eviumstat. A three-electrode cell were used with GCE as a working electrode, platinum wire as a counter electrode, and KCl-saturated Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode. TEM measurement was performed on Philips EM 208S at operating voltage of 100kV. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) measurements were carried out with VEGA 3 SEM. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured by a BRUKER EQUINOX 55 single beam spectrometer. UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded at room temperature on a double beam UV/vis spectrophotometer (OPTIZEN 3220UV).

The hydrothermal method was applied for synthesis of GQDs [45]. Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized using the Hummer’s method with a slight modification [46]. Micrometer-sized graphene sheets (GSs) were gained by thermal deoxidization of graphene oxide sheets in a tube furnace at 300°C for 2h with a heating rate of 5°Cmin− 1 in nitrogen atmosphere. Then 0.05g of these obtained graphene sheets were oxidized in a mixed solution of concentrated H2SO4 (10mL) and HNO3 (30mL) for 20h under mild ultrasonication (300W, 37kHz). The oxidized graphene sheets were diluted and refined with microporous membrane. Then, redispersed in deionized water and the pH was regulated to 8 with Na2CO3. Afterward the suspension was heated at 200°C for 10h in autoclave. The resulting black suspension was filtered with microporous membrane and a brown filter solution was gained. To eliminate larger graphene nanoparticles, the colloidal solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3500Da) for overnight and GQDs were obtained. The powder of graphene quantum dot was obtained by filtration drying in vacuum and characterized by SEM, TEM, UV–vis and FT-IR.

Before each experiment, the GCE was mechanically polished with 0.05μm alumina powder on microcloth pads till a mirror-finish surface was obtained. The electrode was instantly rinsed thoroughly with deionized water, and outright washed successively with anhydrous alcohol and deionized water in an ultrasonic bath, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for the synthesis of 4-(((4-mercaptophenyl)imino)methyl) benzene-1,2-diol (MIB)
ce:section.ce:para: A mixture of 3,4-dihydroxybenzaldehyde (1mmol), 4-aminobenzenethiol (1mmol) and acetonitrile (3mL) was taken in a 5mL round bottomed flask and heated at 70°C temperature. After completion of the reaction (observed on Thin-layer chromatography; n-hexane: ethyl acetate 7:3) the prepared mixture was cooled to room temperature then precipitated product was filtered from mixture by centrifugation and purified using column chromatography. Melting point for MIB was obtained 219–220 °C (Scheme 2 ).

1H and 13C NMR were recorded in DMSO-d6 on a BRUKER DRX-400 AVANCE spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a BRUKER EQUINOX 55 single beam spectrometer at ambient conditions using KBr disks, with a nominal resolution of 4cm−1.

The formation of graphene quantum dots has been confirmed via absorption spectra, FT-IR spectroscopic studies, SEM and TEM measurements at each step of the synthesis (Fig. 2 ). The SEM image of the synthesized GO is given in Fig. 2A. According to the figure, it can be observed that graphene oxide has layered structure. Fig. 2B presents a TEM image of the GQDs. As can be seen, nearly homogeneous nanodisks<50nm diameters are found, most of GQDs were uniformly dispersed and a small amount of GQDs aggregated. Fig. 2C presents the UV–visible absorption spectra of synthesized GO and GQDs. The aqueous suspension of GQDs shows a broad UV–vis absorption spectrum with a shoulder at 290nm, similar to chemically reduced graphene [47]. During the oxidation, oxygen-containing groups, such as carbonyl, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and epoxy groups were introduced to the edges, as shown in the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2D) [48]. The spectrum of powder GQDs illustrates the bands at 1729 (ν (CO)), 1315 (ν (CO)), 1620 (ν (CC)), and abroad band from 2400 to 3400cm−1 (ν (OH)) due to intercalated water molecules present on GQDs.

AuNP solution was characterized by UV–vis spectroscopy. Fig. 3 A presents the UV–vis spectrum of spherical nonaggregated gold nanoparticles is composed by a band around 520nm [49]. SEM image revealed a clear attachment of gold particles onto the FTO surface (Fig. 3B). The average gold particle size determined by SEM was less than 40nm.

Changes in the surface morphology of electrodes, as a result of surface modification, have been examined using scanning electron microscopic technique and the results are displayed in Fig. 3. As can be seen (Fig. 4 , panel B) the GQDs film on the GCE surface is homogeneous, which indicates uniform distribution of the GQDs on the electrode surface. The spherical appearance is found (Fig. 4, panel C) when gold nanoparticles are electropositioned on the surface of ERGQDs/GCE.

EIS is a useful technique providing detailed information on the impedance changes of the electrode interface. In a typical impedance spectrum, a semicircle part at higher frequencies and a straight line at lower frequencies correspond to the electron transfer-limit process and diffusion-limit process, respectively. The charge transfer resistance (R CT) can be estimated by the semicircle diameter of the impedance spectra at high frequency [11]. In order to investigate the conducting nature of GQDs/GCE, ERGQDs/GCE, Au/ERGQDs/GCE and MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE electrodes, electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used. Fig. 5 presents the Nyquist plots obtained for bare GCE, GQDs/GCE, ERGQDs/GCE, Au/ERGQDs/GCE and MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE electrodes in PBS (100mM, pH 7.0, 0.9% NaCl) solution containing 5.0mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. The charge transfer resistance (R CT) can be calculated from the semicircle obtained in the Nyquist plot. An increase in R CT value is observed after the attachment of GQDs on GCE. The insulating nature of GQDs increases the R CT of GQDs/GCE compared to bare GCE. Also, this phenomenon indicates there are electrostatic repulsive interactions between GQDs and [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− anions. After the electrochemical reduction, due to regeneration of sp2 backbone of graphene quantum dot by removing of oxygen functional groups, the R CT value is decreased compared to GQDs/GCE and bare GCE. This phenomenon leads to enhanced electrical conductivity, indicating the facile electron transfer reaction at ERGQDs/GCE electrode. When gold nanoparticles are electrodeposited on the ERGQDs/GCE surface the R CT value is decreased compared to ERGQDs/GCE. Then Au/ERGQDs/GCE was immersed in aqueous solution of 1.0mM MIB for 4h at room temperature. Formation of thiol SAMs on Au/ERGQDs/GCE generally leads to a higher increase in R CT in MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE.

The cyclic voltammetric (CV) studies have been carried out at various potential scan rates (υ, 10–2000mV/s in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.0)) to investigate the interfacial kinetics on the MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE surface (Fig. 6 ). As the scan rate is increased, the anodic and cathodic peaks of electrodes are found to shift in opposite directions, suggesting the redox process to be quasi-reversible. The linear variation of peak potential as a logarithmic function of potential scan rate, in the range from 10 to 2000mV/s, is represented in Fig. 6 (inset A). Using Laviron’s equations, the value of transfer coefficient (α), for n number of electrons, has been determined from the slope of two straight lines with anodic and cathodic slopes of 2.303RT/(1−α)nF and −2.303RT/αnF, respectively. The calculated value for the average transfer coefficient (α) is 0.58. The charge transfer rate constant (k s) has been calculated using Eq. (1) [50]:
(1)
log
k
s
= α log ( 1 - α ) + ( 1 - α ) log α - log ( RT /
n
α
F ν ) - α ( 1 - α )
n
α
F Δ
E
p
/ 2.3 RT
The value of k s =1.35s−1 was evaluated using Eq. (1).

For a surface-controlled process, the surface concentration of electroactive species can be estimated using Eq. (2) [50]:
(2)
I
p
=
n
2
F
2
A Γ ν / 4 RT
where I p is peak current, and Γ is the average surface concentration of the electrode redox substance (molcm−2). From the slope of the anodic peak currents vs. the scan rate (Fig. 6, inset B), the calculated surface concentration is Γ =3.136×10−7 molcm−2 for n =2 and A =(0.0314cm2).

The electrochemical behavior of MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE was studied at different pHs using CV. It was observed that the anodic and cathodic peak potentials of MIB at Au/ERGQDs/GCE shift to negative values by increasing pH. The inset C of Fig. 6 presents potential-pH diagrams by plotting the anodic, cathodic and half-wave potential values as the function of pH. The half-wave potentials (E 1/2) were calculated as the average of the anodic and cathodic peak potentials of the cyclic voltammograms [(E pa + E pc)/2] [51]. It is clearly seen that all the three plots are straight lines with slopes near 63.5mV/pH, indicating that the system obeys the Nernst equation for a two electron and proton transfer reaction [52].

The electroactive surface area (A e) for the modified electrodes can be calculated on the basis of the linear slope of the anodic peak currents vs. the square root of potential sweep rates. For ERGQDs/GCE and Au/ERGQDs/GCE electrodes, A e was obtained by using the calculated diffusion coefficient and a specific concentration of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− via the Randles–Sevick Eq. (3) [52].
(3)
I
p
= ( 2.69 ×
10
5
)
α
1 / 2
n
3 / 2
F
2
ACD
1 / 2
/
ν
1 / 2

Where C is the molar concentration of [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− and A is the electrode surface area, n is the electron transfer number, D is the diffusion coefficient and ν is the scan rate. For the mentioned electrodes, using 5.0mM solution of [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− in PBS (100.0mM, pH=7.0, 0.9% NaCl) and the slope obtained from the linear regression of I p vs. ν 1/2, the A e was calculated. The value of A e for both the ERGQDs/GCE and Au/ERGQDs/GCE were obtained 0.408mm2 and 0.628mm2, respectively. The results indicate that the presence of ERGQDs and gold nanoparticles together causes an increase in active surface of electrodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behaviors of glutathione (GSH) on the MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic ability of the MIB towards the redox of GSH was evaluated at the MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE by CV. Fig. 7 presents the CVs obtained for 0.2mM GSH at bare GCE, GQDs/GCE, ERGQDs/GCE, Au/ERGQDs/GCE and MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE electrodes in 0.1M PBS (pH=7.0). Bare GCE oxidizes GSH at 0.43V (curve b), and the oxidation current decreases along with the potential shift toward more positive potential. Although the oxidation peak current of GSH was enhanced along with the less positive potential shift at GQDs/GCE compared to bare GCE (curve c).

The oxidation peak of GSH after the reduction of GQD in ERGQDs/GCE was shifted to less positive potential with enhanced peak current compared to bare GCE and GQD/GCE (curve d). A significant shift was observed in peak potential for the oxidation of GSH in the case of the Au/ERGQDs/GCE (curve e was obtained with E p =0.30V). On the other hand, the oxidation of GSH was observed at 0.225V with enhanced oxidation current at MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE compared to other electrodes (curve g).

Comparison of curve (e) and (g) presents the anodic peak current at the surface of MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE is greatly enhanced by addition of GSH to the solution and the cathodic peak current completely disappear.

These results suggested that an electrocatalytic behavior for GSH oxidation at the surface of MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE via an EC catalytic mechanism and also MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE was highly suitable for the determination of GSH.

The effect of scan rate on the electrocatalytic oxidation of GSH at the surface of MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE was investigated by CV (Fig. 8 ). The oxidation peak potential of GSH at MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE shifted to more positive potentials with increasing scan rate. Furthermore, a plot of peak current (I p) vs. the square root of scan rate (ν 1/2) was found to be linear in the range of 5–50mVs−1, suggesting that the electrocatalytic process is controlled by GSH diffusion to the electrode surface (inset A of Fig. 8). A plot of the scan rate-normalized current (I p/ν 1/2) vs. scan rate (inset B of Fig. 8) exhibits the characteristic typical shape of an EĆ process [52].

The inset C of Fig. 8 presents the Tafel plot for the sharp rising part of the current–voltage curve. A Tafel slope of 0.149V decade−1 was obtained which agrees well with the involvement of one electron in the rate determining step of the electrode process [52], assuming a transfer coefficient, of 0.48.

To examine the sensitivity of MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE, the chronoamperometric method was used. Chronoamperometric measurements of GSH at MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE were carried out at the working electrode potential of 200mV for various concentrations of GSH (Fig. 9 ). For an electroactive material (GSH in this case) with a diffusion coefficient of D, the current observed for the electrochemical reaction at the mass transport limited condition is described by the Cottrell equation [52]. Experimental plots of I vs. t − 1/2 were employed, with the best fits for different concentrations of GSH (inset A of Fig. 9). The slopes of the resulting straight lines were then plotted vs. GSH concentration (inset B of Fig. 9). From the resulting slope and Cottrell equation the mean value of the D was found to be (1.8×10− 5 cm2/s). Chronoamperometry can also be employed to evaluate the catalytic rate constant, k, for the reaction between GSH and MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE according to the method of Galus (Eq. (4)) [53].
(4)
I
C
/
I
L
=
γ
1 / 2
[
π
1 / 2
erf (
γ
1 / 2
) + exp ( - γ ) /
γ
1 / 2
]
where I C is the catalytic current of GSH at the MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE, I L is the limited current in the absence of GSH and γ = kC b t is the argument of the error function (C b is the bulk concentration of GSH). In cases where γ exceed the value of 2, the error function is almost equal to 1 and therefore, the above equation can be reduced to Eq. (5):
(5)
I
C
/
I
L
=
π
1 / 2
γ
1 / 2
=
π
1 / 2
(
kC
b
t )
1 / 2
where t is the time elapsed. The above equation can be used to calculate the rate constant, k, of the catalytic process from the slope of I C/I L vs. t 1/2 at a given GSH concentration. From the values of the slopes, the average value of k was found to be (9.40±0.15)×10+2 M−1 S−1).

The improved characteristics have been obtained for the fabricated electrode may be attributed to the high charge detaching efficiency of ERGQDs and Au nanoparticles that promote electron transfer from the electrode surface to GSH. Due to providing high surface to volume ratio, these results are expected from ERGQDs and Au nanoparticles. Thus increasing the effective surface area available for signal transduction and quantity improvement of self-assembled monolayer of MIB, ultimately lead to the improved detection limit.

In order to investigate the repeatability of MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE, the oxidation of 0.2mM GSH was recorded using DPV for 8 measurements at physiological pH solution. The RSD calculated for DPV peak current is about 1.9%.

For evaluation the reproducibility of the results, five different MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE electrodes were fabricated under similar conditions. Their current response and peak potential toward the oxidation of GSH were the same at all five electrodes. The calculated RSD for DPV peak current is about 2.2% that indicate the reproducibility of electrode preparation is acceptable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous determination of GSH, UA and Trp
ce:section.ce:para: Due to disability of bare GCE electrode in separating of electrochemical response in simultaneous measurement of GSH, UA and Trp, the modified electrode (MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE) was employed which greatly increased the selectivity for determination of these analytes and the oxidation peaks were well separated. This may be attributed to the high surface to volume ratio of ERGQDs and Au nanoparticles and also electrocatalytic activity of MIB toward GSH. Therefore, DPV was used for the simultaneous determination of GSH, UA and Trp at MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE. Well-defined oxidation peaks were observed for GSH, UA and Trp at 0.06, 0.31 and 0.55V vs. Ag/AgCl, respectively (Fig. 11 ). This result suggested that the MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE was highly suitable for the simultaneous determination of the GSH, UA and Trp in a mixture.

By increasing concentrations of GSH, UA and Trp, the peak currents of the respective analytes were increased linearly. The corresponding peak current vs. analyte concentration (calibration plots) for GSH, UA and Trp are shown in insets of Fig. 11. The sensitivity of the modified electrode towards the oxidation of GSH in the presence of UA and Trp (0.4722 and 0.0361μAμM− 1) is very close to the value observed in the absence of UA and Trp (0.5027 and 0.0357 μA μM− 1). Therefore the oxidation processes of GSH, UA and Trp at MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE are independent and the simultaneous determination of the three analytes is possible without any interference. The detection limit and linear range are obtained as 5.5μM and 15.0–1100.0μM for UA and 6.5μM and 20.0–1000.0μM for Trp.

The influence of various foreign species on the determination of 0.1mM GSH was investigated. The tolerance limit was taken as the maximum concentration of the foreign substances, which caused an approximately ±5% relative error in the determination. The tolerated concentration of foreign substances was 1.0×10−1 M for Na+, K+, Ca2+, SO4 2−, NO3−, Cl−, Br−, S2−, CO3−, CH3COO− and 20-fold excess of alanine, arginine, aspartic acid, citric acid, folic acid, l-asparagine, l-lysine, glutamic acid, serine, threonine, valine, leucine, glycine, l-tryptophan and l-tyrosine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE for recovery and determination of GSH in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the analytical applicability of the proposed MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE, the electrode was employed to determination of GSH, UA and Trp in synthetic solutions and diluted blood serum samples by standard addition calibration method. Five synthetic mixture solutions containing different concentrations of GSH, UA and Trp were prepared by phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). Each of these samples was analyzed by DPV five times and average recovery percent was obtained.

Also, different amounts of GSH spiked to three diluted serum samples (diluted with phosphate buffer solution pH 7.0). Then the prepared samples were analyzed by the proposed electrochemical sensor five times. The recovery results for determination of GSH in synthetic solutions and serum samples are given in Table 2 , which shows satisfactory recovery percent.

The concentrations of GSH, UA and Trp in serum samples were analyzed using the HPLC technique [54–57] and the results agreed with those values measured using the MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE electrode. Results are also given in Table 2. These results indicate that determination of GSH in blood serum samples in the presence of certain amounts of UA and Trp can be successfully performed by the proposed novel method.

The present work demonstrated a simple, facile, and reproducible method for the modification of GCE by graphene quantum dot (GQDs), gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and 4-(((4-mercaptophenyl)imino)methyl)benzene-1,2-diol (MIB). Combination of GQDs and gold nanoparticles can increase electrode surface and enhance electronic and catalytic properties. According to the experimental data, MIB/Au/ERGQDs/GCE has quite high efficiency and shows electrocatalytic activity toward GSH. Also, the application of this modified electrode in analysis of real samples for simultaneous determination of GSH, UA and Trp was evaluated with highly sensitivity and good performance. Moreover, the HPLC analysis used for comparative purpose. Results showed that a certain amount of GSH, UA and Trp found in the samples. These results indicate that determination of GSH in blood serum samples in the presence of certain amounts of UA and Trp can be successfully performed by the proposed novel method. Moreover, the electrochemical response of present modified electrode does not change appreciably, so it can be used for the fabrication of stable sensors for the selective determination of electroactive analytes. Well-defined oxidation peaks were observed for GSH, UA and Trp at 0.06, 0.31 and 0.55V vs. Ag/AgCl, respectively in mixtures without significant interferences.
There is no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Yazd University Research Council, IUT Research Council and Excellence in Sensors for financial support of this research.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Int. J. Radiat. Oncol. Biol. Phys.
Electrochemical Methods: Fundamentals and Applica tions
